Facts.text: Battle of Rudau: Teutonic Knights of Germany heavily defeat the Grand Duchy of Lithuania north of Königsberg
Battle of Hemmingstedt: German peasant army of Dithmarschen repels King John of Denmark and his brother Duke Frederick's army of Schleswig and Holstein
Boris Godunov chosen as Tsar of Russia
Italian philosopher Giordano Bruno is burned alive at Campo de' Fiori in Rome, convicted of heresy by the Roman Inquisition
Puritan author William Prynne tried in Star Chamber for publishing "Histrio-masti", criticising the theatre
France and Bavaria sign military assistance treaty
Ambon earthquake and megatsunami kills at least 2322 people in the Maluku Islands (modern Indonesia), tsunami reaches height of 100 meters (330 ft)
Thomas Neale granted English patent for American postal service
1st Partition of Poland signed in Vienna by Austria, Prussia and Russia
Battle of Mormant: French Imperial army defeat Russian Austrian forces (War of the Sixth Coalition)
Baron Karl von Drais de Sauerbrun patents the "draisine," an early bicycle
Tuscany gets liberal Constitution
Britain recognises independence of Orange Free State (South Africa)
Confederate submarine CSS H.L. Hunley sinks Union ship USS Housatonic at Charleston, South Carolina in the world's first successful submarine attack; crews of both vessels were killed
-18] Battle of Charleston, South Carolina
Gyula Andrássy is appointed as the first Prime Minister of the newly separated Kingdom of Hungary by King Franz Joseph I
Sardines first canned by Julius Wolff in Eastport, Maine
1st telephone exchange in San Francisco, California opens with 18 phones
Australian cricket opening bowlers Joey Palmer (7/68) and Edwin Evans (3/64) dismiss England for 133 in 2nd Test; first Test match played at Sydney Cricket Ground
Arthur Ashwell patents 'vacant/engaged' toilet lock in London, England
Muzzling Order on the London County Council enforced
National Organization of Mothers formed in American by Alice McLellan Birney and Phoebe Apperson Hearst (Parent Teacher Association)
A general strike in Barcelona and nearby towns leads to government-troop reprisals that leave 40 dead
American educator and women's suffragist Frances Willard becomes 1st women honored in National Statuary Hall in the US Capital building, Washington, D.C.

(Glenn Curtiss demonstrates his Triad hydroplane's flight to and from a ship, landing alongside USS Pennsylvania, and having the craft lifted aboard by crane in San Diego Bay, California: after lunch, the plane was returned to the water and took off for land
US State of Oregon becomes second to enact minimum wage law
Edward Stone, 1st US combatant to die in WW I, is mortally wounded
In Australia, Nationalist Party takes over a coalition government
Avalanche buries 75 in Sap Gulch, Bingham, Utah; 40 die
Deems Taylor's opera "The King's Henchman", with libretto by Edna St. Vincent Millay, premieres at the Metropolitan Opera, NYC
Toronto Maple Leafs, in the team's first game since changing name from "St. Patricks", beat the visiting New York Americans, 4-1 at the Mutual Street Arena
American brothers, Jennison and John Heaton fight out the top placings in the cresta (now known as skeleton) at the St. Moritz Winter Olympics; Jennison takes the gold by 1.0s
Johan Grøttumsbråten leads a Norwegian sweep of the medals in the 18k cross country at the St. Moritz Winter Olympics; he also wins the Nordic combined gold the following day
French government of André Tardieu falls for the first time
Hockey's Hershey Bears (now with AHL) 1st game
First issue of American news magazine "Newsweek" is published
US Senate accepts Blaine Act: ending prohibition
1st high school auto driving course offered (State College, Pennsylvania)
-58°F (-50°C), McIntosh, South Dakota (state record)
S. N. Behrman's play "End of Summer" premieres in NYC
The world's first superhero, The Phantom, a cartoon strip by Lee Falk, makes his first appearance in comics
First public experimental demonstration of Baird color TV occurs in London
Katwijk soccer team forms in the Netherlands

Altmark Incident: Crew of the British destroyer "Cossack" board German "Altmark" in Jøssingfjord, Norway, releasing 299 British prisoners after hand-to-hand fighting with bayonets and the last recorded Royal Naval action with cutlass
Dutch churches protest at Seyss-Inquart against persecution of Jews
Operation Hailstone: US begins night bombing of Truk island in the Pacific
Humanistic Covenant forms in Amsterdam
Dutch RC bishops publish manifest against "godless communism"
Voice of America begins broadcasting to USSR
Chaim Weitzman elected 1st president of Israel
Ice Pairs Championship at Paris won by Andrea Kekesy and Ede Kiraly of Hungary
Ladies' Figure Skating Champions in Paris won by Alena Vrzanova of CZE
Two Long Island Rail Road trains collide head-on, killing 32 near Rockville Centre, Long Island, New York
Montreal Canadiens center Elmer Lach picks up his 354th career assist in a 3-2 loss at the NY Rangers to become NHL's all-time assists leader; passes Bill Cowley's mark
Trude Jochum-Beiser of Austria wins the downhill gold medal at the Oslo Winter Olympics; second career gold medal after taking out the combined event at St. Moritz (1948)
DSB soccer team forms in Eindhoven (now PSV Eindhoven), Netherlands
WAST (now WNYT) TV channel 13 in Albany-Troy, NY (NBC) 1st broadcast
American golfer Mike Souchak sets a PGA 72-hole record of 257, which remains unbroken until 2001
Ice Dance Championship at Vienna Austria won by J Westwood/Demmy GRB
Ice Pairs Championship at Vienna won by Frances Dafoe and Norris Bowden of Canada
KTVF TV channel 11 in Fairbanks, AK (CBS/ABC) begins broadcasting
Ice Dance Championship at Garmisch won by Pamela Weight/P Thomas GRB
Ice Pairs Championship at Garmisch won by Schwarz & Oppelt of AUT
A fire at a home for the elderly in Warrenton, Missouri kills 72 people.
Comic strip "BC" 1st appears
WETV (now WPBA) TV channel 30 in Atlanta, GA (PBS) begins broadcasting
1st weather satellite launched, Vanguard 2, 9.8 kg
Storm in Hamburg kills 265
Japanese runner Toru Terasawa runs a world record marathon (2:15:15.8) at the Beppu Marathon on the island of Kyushu, Japan
101st member elected to Baseball Hall of Fame, Chicago White Sox shortstop Luke Appling
US House of Representatives accept Law on the civil rights
US Supreme Court rules - 1 man 1 vote (Westberry v Sanders)
WMEM TV channel 10 in Presque Isle, ME (PBS) begins broadcasting
US Ranger 8 launched, will transmit 7,137 lunar pictures
French satellite Diapason D-1A launch into Earth orbit
Kosmos 140 (Soyuz Test) launches into Earth orbit
Parlophone releases the Beatles' double "A" side single "Penny Lane" and "Strawberry Fields" in the UK

Frenchman Jean Claude-Killy completes a clean sweep of the 
alpine skiing events at the Grenoble Winter Olympics when he wins the slalom; also takes out the downhill and giant slalom
Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame, Springfield, Massachusetts opens
Norwegian cross country skier Ole Ellefsæter wins 50k event at the Grenoble Winter Olympics; 2nd gold medal of the Games (4 x 10k relay)
Soviet Union wins its 3rd Olympic ice hockey gold medal in Grenoble; clinch title with final round, 5-0 win over Canada; Soviet LW Anatoli Firsov top scores with 16 points
"Cloud Nine" 9th studio album by The Temptations is released (Grammy Award Best Rhythm & Blues Performance by a Duo or Group 1969, Billboard Album of the Year 1969)
Robert Marasco's "Child's Play" premieres in NYC
US army officer Jeffrey MacDonald murders his pregnant wife and two small daughters
Rodney Redmond scores 107 on debut v Pakistan, his only Test Cricket
49 die in stampede for seats at soccer match, Cairo, Egypt
Robert K. Preston, a disgruntled U.S. Army private, buzzes the White House with a stolen helicopter.
Macau adopts constitution (Organic Law of Macau)
NZ scores their 1st innings win in Test Cricket, v India
11 civilians and 1 RUC officer are killed and 30 wounded by a Provisional Irish Republican Army incendiary bomb at the La Mon Restaurant near Belfast
China invades Vietnam, marking the start of the Sino-Vietnamese War
Derrynaflan Hoard of five priceless 8th/9th century liturgical vessels discovered with metal detectors on Derrynaflan Island, County Tipperary - one of Ireland's most exciting archaeological finds
Sapporo downhill silver medallist Annemarie Moser-Pröll of Austria goes one better with gold at the Lake Placid Winter Olympics

Swedish cross-country skier Thomas Wassberg earns first of 4 career Olympic gold medals in the 15k at Lake Placid; wins by 0.01s from Finland's Juha Mieto; closest margin of victory ever in Olympic cross-country skiing
Chrysler Corporation reports largest corporate losses in US history
Commencement of Sri Lanka's 1st Test Cricket match, v England
Bob Bourne fails on 8th Islander penalty shot
Netherlands adopts constitution
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site

Biathletes Eirik Kvalfoss of Norway and West German Peter Angerer finish 2nd & 3rd respectively in the relay at the Sarajevo Winter Olympics; both have complete sets of medals from biathlon events at the Games
1st class postage rises from 20 cents to 22 cents
1st day/night game at the MCG, Australia v England
3rd person to receive an artificial heart (Murray Haydon)
Dutch speed skater Hein Vergeer becomes World Allround champion
1st Francophone Summit convenes at Versailles
Howard Stern Radio Show returns to NYC morning radio (WXRK 92.3 FM)
Johnson & Johnson announces it will no longer sell capsule drugs
Libyan bombers attack N'djamena Airport in Chad
US Lt Col William Higgins kidnapped in south Lebanon by Lebanese terrorists & later killed
6-week study of Arctic atmosphere shows no ozone "hole"
Mauritania, Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia & Libya form common market
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
33rd Daytona 500: Ernie Irvan, driving for Morgan-McClure Motorsports coasts to win after challengers crash on finals laps
Haitian ferry boat capsize in storm, 800-2,000 die
Hartford right wing Pat Verbeek scores his 300th career NHL goal and adds his 300th career assist in a 5-3 Whalers' home loss to the Buffalo Sabres
"The Brady Bunch Movie", based on the 1970s TV series, premieres in America
Colin Fergusson found guilty of killing 6 people on the Long Island Rail Road in NY
Federal judge allows lawsuit claiming US tobacco makers knew nicotine was addictive & manipulated its levels to keep customers hooked
Weekly Standard shows evidence Larry Flint sexually abused his daughter
Larry Wayne Harris & Bill Levitt arrested for possession of anthrax
NASA's Voyager 1 spacecraft overtakes Pioneer 10 to become the farthest object from earth in space
US Naval Academy cadet Diane Zamora, 20, convicted of capital murder
USA Women's Ice Hockey Team beats Canada and wins the first Olympic Gold medal
44th Daytona 500: Ward Burton's win first for Dodge sinceRichard Petty's victory in the 1974 race
Westlife go to No.1 on the UK singles chart with 'World Of Our Own.' The Irish boy band's 10th UK No.1 single.
The London Congestion Charge scheme begins in London, England
A massive mudslide occurs in Southern Leyte, Philippines, killing 1,126 people
50th Daytona 500: Ryan Newman wins shootout going into the final turn from Tony Stewart, and the Busch brothers, Kurt and Kyle
Kosovo declares independence from Serbia
Approximately 70 ancient Olympic artifacts are stolen from the Archaeological Museum of Greece
Friso, Prince of Orange-Nassau (31), buried under an avalanche while skiing in Lech, Austria; suffers oxygen deprivation and lapses into a terminal coma
The President of Germany, Christian Wulff, resigns over a corruption scandal
37 people are killed and 130 are injured in a series of Baghdad car bombings
5 people are killed and 11 are injured after a gas explosion destroyed an apartment complex in Frenštát pod Radhoštěm, Czech Republic
President Rafael Correa wins the Ecuadorian general election in a landslide victory
American pair Meryl Davis and Charlie White win the first Olympic gold medal for the US in ice dance at the Sochi Winter Games
Belarus takes the aerials double at the Sochi Winter Olympics when Anton Kushnir wins the men's event; Alla Tsuper claims the women's section
Belarusian biathlete Darya Domracheva wins her 3rd gold medal of the Sochi Winter Olympics in the mass start; also wins individual and pursuit gold
Car bomb attack on military convoy in Ankara, Turkey, by Kurdish militant eaves 28 dead
Channing Dungey announced new President of ABC Entertainment Group, first African-American to lead a major US broadcast network

Oldest known case of human-Neanderthal sex (100,000 years ago) is revealed by the Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology in Leipzig when a 50,000-year-old Neanderthal woman's remains from the Altai Mountains show traces of Homo sapien DNA
Discovery of a new mostly underwater continent, Zealandia, in the South Pacific is announced in the research journal "GSA Today"
10k freestyle cross country champion Ragnhild Haga and 30k classical winner Marit Bjørgen win their 2nd Olympic gold medal in Pyeongchang as part of Norway's 4 x 5k relay team
3 suicide bombers kill 18 in Konduga, north-east Nigeria
Britain's Lizzy Yarnold wins her 2nd consecutive Olympic women's skeleton gold medal in Pyeongchang; also wins event in Sochi (2014)
Czech skier Ester Ledecká wins super-G gold medal at Pyeongchang Winter Olympics; later wins parallel giant slalom snowboarding to be first female to win gold in different sports at a single Games
Polish ski jumper Kamil Stoch retains his Olympic individual large hill title (Sochi 2014) with gold in Pyeongchang; beats normal hill winner Andreas Wellinger of Germany by 3.4m
Russian biathlete Anastasiya Kuzmina wins her 3rd career Olympic gold medal in women's 12.5k mass start in Pyeongchang; 7.5k sprint champion in Vancouver (2010) and Sochi (2014)
Protests continue by thousands in streets of Port-au-Prince, Haiti, against government corruption and inflation
62nd Daytona 500: Denny Hamlin wins second straight title by 0.014 seconds over Ryan Blaney on the second restart in overtime; his third Daytona victory
General Motors announces it is retiring the Holden brand in Australasia
India's Supreme Court grants equal rights to women in the armed forces
South Africa, Africa's worst-affected country begins COVID-19 vaccinations with the one-shot Johnson & Johnson vaccine in Cape Town
28,000 women apply for 30 jobs driving trains after they are advertised for women in Saudi Arabia for the first time
Global LGBT festival WorldPride opens in Sydney, Australia, its first time in the Southern Hemisphere
Ukraine withdraws its troops from the key eastern town of Avdiivka, blaming a lack of western-supplied weapons
An airplane crashes and flips over at Toronto airport, injuring 21 people, the fourth major aviation accident in North America in a month
Wars of the Roses: Second Battle of St Albans - Lancastrian army defeats Yorkists and recaptures KingHenry VI
Portuguese admiralAfonso de Albuquerquefirst conquers the city of Goa, entering it with little conflict

Treaty of Adrianople: Representatives of Holy Roman EmperorMaximilian IIand Ottoman SultanSelim IIagree to a peace plan ending the war between the Habsburg monarchy and the Ottoman Empire; Maximilian agrees to provide a cash "present" and ruling authority is granted to the Ottomans in Transylvania, Moldavia, and Wallachia
Myles Standishis elected as the first commander of the Plymouth Colony
1st volume ofEdward Gibbon's seminal work "The Decline and Fall of Roman Empire" published
French astronomerCharles Messiercatalogs M83 (spiral galaxy in Hydra)

Charles Lyellmakes his presidential address to the Geographical Society, London and announces that Richard Owen has concluded from Darwin's fossils that extinct species were related to current species in the same locality
Giuseppe Verdi's opera "Un Ballo in maschera" premieres in Naples
Alexander IIof Russia survives an assassination attempt
Otto von Bismarckgives Carl Peters' firm management of German possessions in East-Africa
Emilio Aguinaldoand a group of katipuneros defeat Spanish forces led by General Camilo de Polavieja at the Battle of Zapote Bridge in Cavite
Giacomo Puccini's opera "Madama Butterfly" premieres at La Scala in Milan, Italy
New York Armory Show introducesPablo Picasso,Henri Matisse, andMarcel Duchampto US public
Arthur Honegger's chamber piece "Pastorale D'ete" premieres
Ottawa left wingCy Dennenypasses Joe Malone as all-time NHL goal scoring leader; scores his 143rd career goal in Senators' 2-0 win over Montreal Canadiens
American swimmerJohnny Weissmullersets 100m world freestyle record 57.4s at Miami, Florida
In his 14th title defenseJoe LouisKOs Gus Dorazio in the 2nd round at the Convention Center, Philadelphia to retain NYSAC heavyweight boxing crown
Adolf Hitlervisits field marshal Erich von Manstein's headquarters in Zaporozje, Ukraine and stays until the 19th
Dow Chemical and Corning Glass Works form a joint venture to explore and produce silicon materials, based upon the work ofJames Franklin Hyde
Major GeneralOmar Bradleyflies to Washington, D.C.
NY YankeeJoe DiMaggioenlists into the US army
Detroit right wingGordie Howescores his 100th career NHL goal and adds an assist in a 2-1 Red Wings' victory over the Canadiens, in Montreal, Quebec
US-Japan baseball relations suspended overMasanori Murakamidispute
Bob Dylanand Johnny Cash record an album; it is never released
Italian tenorLuciano Pavarottireceives record 17 curtain calls after his performance in "La fille du régiment" at New York's Metropolitan Opera
US PresidentRichard Nixonleaves Washington, D.C. for a groundbreaking trip to China
US National Security AdviserHenry Kissingermeets Chinese leaderMao Zedong, where the latter jokingly offers to send 10 million Chinese women to the United States
16th Daytona 500:Richard Pettyfirst driver to win back-to-back titles at Daytona; his 5th Great American race
A&M Records releasesJohn Lennon's sixth studio album, "Rock 'n' Roll" album, cover songs of early rock numbers
Richard Hadleetakes 7-23 New Zealand v India, his 1st match-winning spell
Kalakuta Republic, commune of Nigerian musician and activistFela Kuti, burnt to the ground by soldiers in Lagos
American speed skaterEric Heidenequals world record 1000m (1:14.99)
22nd Daytona 500: Buddy Baker wins ahead ofBobby Allisonand Neil Bonnett
27th Daytona 500: After taking pole at then-record speed of 205.114 mphBill Elliottdominates race leading 136 of 200 laps
Colombian drug lord the 'Godmother of Cocaine'Griselda Blancoarrested in Irvine, California - later convicted and serves 15 years for manufacturing and distributing cocaine
Panamanian jockeyLaffit PincayJr is third to ride 6,000 winners; wins aboard Doria's Delight in the 5th race at Santa Anita Park
New York YankeesDon Mattinglywins highest salary arbitration ($1,975,000 per year)
Former baseball player and managerLeo Durocherinjured in a car crash
Tiger ManagerSparky Andersontakes unpaid leave due to baseball strike
Garry Kasparovdefeats chess-playing computer Deep Blue 4-2
'Always On Time' by R&B artistJa Rulefeaturing Ashanti starts a two-week run at #1 on the US singles chart.
"If I Ain't Got You" single released byAlicia Keys(Grammy Award Best Female R&B Vocal Performance 2005, Billboard Song of the Year 2004)
Thierry Henryreturns to Red Bulls from his loan at Arsenal
"The Tonight Show StarringJimmy Fallon" premieres on NBC
US Secretary of StateJohn Kerryclaims climate change requires urgent action and that only a small "window of time" remained open
Chief executiveTim Cookconfirms Apple will contest an FBI order to unlock the phone of San Bernardino gunman Syed Rizwan Farook
Nike ends endorsement deal with Filipino boxerManny Pacquiaoafter he made TV comments gay people are "worse than animals."
Japanese defending championYuzuru Hanyuretains his Olympic men's figure skating title by winning the gold medal at the Pyeongchang Winter Olympics; first wins gold at Sochi (2014)
61st Daytona 500:Joe GibbsRacing goes 1-2-3; Denny Hamlin holds off reigning Cup Series champion Joey Logano and teammateKyle Buschto win his 2nd Great American Race
Amazon bossJeff Bezospledges $10 billion to help fight climate change
Laureus World Sports Awards, Berlin, Germany: Sportsman:Lewis Hamilton&Lionel Messi; Sportswoman:Simone Biles; Team: South Africa National Rugby Union team
Texan senatorTed Cruzflies to Cancun, Mexico with his family amid a winter disaster in his state, igniting widespread condemnation
US House of Representatives breaks electoral college tie by electingThomas Jeffersonas President over Aaron Burr
Treaty of Ghent ratified by the US Senate and signed by PresidentJames Madisonending War of 1812, over a month after it was signed in Europe

Battle of Miani: Bombay Army of the East India Company led byCharles Napierdefeats a Baluch army of Talpur Emirs of Sindh led by Mir Nasir Khan Talpur; the company captures parts of Sindh, their first possession in modern-day Pakistan

Union forces led byWilliam T. Shermancapture the state capital of Columbia, South Carolina. The city is ablaze by nightfall, fanned by high winds devastating much of the city. It is not known which side started the fires.
Battle of Eniwetok begins with US forces landing on Canna and Camelia islets in the Pacific
British Parliament votes to join the European Economic Community
Sales of the Volkswagen Beetle model exceed those of Ford Model T
Rudolphus Agricola [Roelof Huysman], Dutch humanist, scholar (De inventione dialectica), poet, and organist, born in Baflo, Groningen, the Hanseatic League (d. 1485) (alternate date 1443)
François, de Lorraine, 2nd Duke of Guise, French soldier famous for the defense of Metz and statesman as Grand Chamberlain of France, born in Bar, France (d. 1563)
Fausto Poli, Italian Catholic Cardinal. private secretary to Pope Urban, born in Usigni (d. 1653)
Johann Henry Alting, German theologian, born in Emden, Saxony (d. 1644)
Pierre Le Pesant, sieur de Boisguilbert, French economist, born in Rouen, France (d. 1714)
Arcangelo Corelli, Italian composer and violinist (Concerti Grossi), born in Fusignano, Duchy of Modena and Reggio (d. 1713)
Georg Bronner, German composer and organist, baptized in Hamburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1720)
Johann Melchior Conradi, German composer, born in Oettingen, County of Oettingen, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1756)
Ernst Gottlieb Baron, German lutenist and composer, born in Breslau, Silesia (d. 1760)
Louis-Maurice de La Pierre, French composer, born in Versailles, France (d. 1753)
Georg Wenzeslaus von Knobelsdorff, Prussian architect (Sanssouci) and painter, born in Kuckädel, Poland (d. 1753)
Matthew Tilghman, American delegate to the Continental Congress, born on The Hermitage plantation, Maryland (d. 1790)
Tobias Mayer, German astronomer "method of lunars" for longitude determination, born in Marbach (d. 1762)
Horace Bénédict de Saussure, Swiss physicist and geologist, born in Conches, Switzerland (d. 1799)
Narciso Casanovas, Spanish keyboard player, composer, and Benedictine monk, born in Sabadell, Spain (d. 1799)
Friedrich Maximilian von Klinger, German playwright (Der Wirrwarr, oder Sturm und Drang) and novelist, born in the Free Imperial City of Frankfurt (d. 1831)
Jan Jachym Kopriva, Czech composer and organist, born in Cítoliby, Kingdom of Bohemia, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1792)
John Pinkerton, Scottish anti-Celtic historian, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1826)
Raphaelle Peale, American painter (After the Rain, 1823), born in Annapolis, Maryland (d. 1825)
René Laënnec, French physician who invented the stethoscope, born in Quimper, France (d. 1826)
Giovanni Pacini, Italian opera composer (Saffo), and educator, born in Catania, Kingdom of Sicily (d. 1867)
Philipp Franz von Siebold, German physician and botanist who introduced Western medicine to Japan, born in Würzburg (d. 1866)
Samuel Read Anderson, American soldier in the Mexican-American War and Confederate general, born in Bedford County, Virginia (d. 1883)
Friedrich Wilhelm Markull, German composer and musician, born in Reichenbach, Prussia (d. 1887)
Haller Nutt, American rich Southern Plantation owner (Longwood house - largest octagonal house in the US), born at Laurel Hill Plantation, Mississippi (d. 1864)
Elzéar-Alexandre Taschereau, Catholic cardinal, Archbishop of Quebec, born in Sainte-Marie-de-la-Nouvelle-Beauce, Lower Canada (d. 1898)
Henri Vieuxtemps, Belgian violin prodigy, composer, and teacher (Brussels Conservatory), born in Verviers, United Kingdom of the Netherlands (d. 1881)
Lola Montez, Irish dancer and courtesan, born in Grange, Ireland (d. 1861)
William Farrar "Baldy" Smith, American Major General (Union Army), born in St. Albans, Vermont (d. 1903)
Francisco Salvador Daniel, French composer, and ethnomusicologist, born in Bourges, France (d. 1871)
Gustavo Adolfo Bécquer, Spanish Romantic poet (Rimas y leyendas), born in Seville, Spain (d. 1870)
Francis Jay Herron, American Major General (Union Army), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1902)
Samuel van Houten, Dutch politician and philosopher (child labor laws), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1930)
Aaron Montgomery Ward, American retailer, founded mail-order business (Montgomery Ward), born in Chatham, New Jersey (d. 1913)
Louisa Lawson, Australian writer, publisher (The Dawn) and suffragist who founded The Dawn Club, born in Gulgong, New South Wales (d. 1920)
Selwyn Image, English painter and designer (Arts and Crafts Movement), born in Bodiam Sussex (d. 1930)
Anton Urspruch, German composer, born in Free City of Frankfurt, German Confederation (d. 1907)
Ludwig Bonvin, Swiss born composer who worked in America, born in Sierre, Switzerland (d. 1939)
Friedrich Alfred Krupp, German steel and arms manufacturer (Krupp AG - supplied the German Navy), born in Essen, Province of Westphalia (d. 1902)
Aleksander Różycki, Polish pianist, composer, and music teacher, born in Zhitomir, Russian Empire (d. 1915)
Otto Liman von Sanders, German general in Turkey during WWI, born in Stolp, Prussia (d. 1929)
Samuel Sidney McClure, Irish-American newspaper editor, publisher and muckracker (McClure's Magazine), born in County Antrim, Ireland (d. 1949)
Ernest Ford, English composer, born in Warminster, Wiltshire, England (d. 1919)
Helena of Waldeck and Pyrmont, German princess and Duchess of Albany, born in Arolsen, Waldeck and Pyrmont (d. 1922)
Edward German (Jones), English composer (Merrie England), born in Whitchurch, Shropshire, England (d. 1936)
Mori Ōgai, Japanese novelist (The Wild Geese), poet and surgeon, born in Iwami Province, Japan (d. 1922)
Fyodor Sologub, Russian Symbolist novelist, poet and translator, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1927)
(Andrew) "Banjo" Paterson, Australian journalist, poet (Clancy of the Overflow), and lyricist ("Waltzing Matilda"), born near Orange, New South Wales, Australia (d. 1941)
Jozef Murgaš, Slovak priest and inventor who achieved radiotelegraph transmission between Wilkes-Barre and Scranton in 1905, born in Tajov, Austria-Hungary (d. 1929)
William Cadbury, British chocolate manufacturer (Cadbury), born in Edgbaston, England (d. 1957)
Carlos Viegas Gago Coutinho, Portuguese cartographer and aviator (1st flight across the south Atlantic), born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1959)
Louis de Raet, Belgian economist and founder of the Flemish People's Party, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1914)
André Maginot, French politician, advocated for the Maginot Line, born in Paris, France (d. 1932)
Henri Vandeputte, Belgian author and poet (L'homme Jeune), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1952)
Isabelle Eberhardt, Swiss explorer and writer of North Africa, born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1904)
Dorothy Canfield Fisher, American novelist and social reformer (managed 1st US adult education program, helped conscientious objectors), born in Lawrence, Kansas (d. 1958)
Kurt Schindler, German-American composer and conductor, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1935)
Arthur Vanderpoorten, Belgian politician (Minister of Internal Affairs 1940), born in Puurs, Belgium (d. 1945)
Leevi Antti Madetoja, Finnish composer (Pohjalaisia), conductor and teacher, born in Oulu, Finland, (d. 1947)
Henrietta "Hetty" Beck, Dutch hearing actress (Bouwmeester Award), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1979)
Otto Stern, German-American physicist (Stern-Gerlach-experiment, Nobel 1943), born in Sohrau, Prussia, German Empire (d. 1969)
Ronald Arbuthnott Knox, British priest, broadcaster and author (Viaduct Murder), born in Kibworth Leicestershire, England (d. 1957)
H[aroldson] L Hunt, Texas oil tycoon (East Texas Oil Field), born near Ramsey, Illinois (d. 1974)
Georg Britting, German poet and writer (Fratricide in the Backwater), born in Regensburg, Germany (d. 1964)
Theodor Plievier, German writer (Des Kaisers Kulis [The Kaiser's Coolies]; Stalingrad), born in Berlin, German Empire (d. 1955)
Wally Pipp, American baseball first baseman (World Series 1923; AL HR leader 1916, 17; NY Yankees), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1965)
Anita Stewart, American silent film actress (South of Hell Mountain), producer, and mystery novelist, born in Brooklyn, NYC (d. 1961)
Edna Park Edwards, American stage and vaudeville actress, born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1967)
Tom Lowry, New Zealand cricket batsman (7 Tests, 2 x 50s; first NZ Test captain), President NZ Thoroughbred Breeders Association 1951-65, born in Fernhill, New Zealand (d. 1976)
Hans Morgenthau, German-American political scientist, international relationship expert, and author (Politics Among Nations), born in Coburg, German Empire (d. 1980)
Luis A. Ferré, Puerto Rican industrialist and politician (Governor of Puerto Rico, 1969-73), born in Ponce, Puerto Rico (d. 2003)
Andy Oberlander, American College Football Hall of Fame halfback (Dartmouth College; National C'ship 1954), born in Chelsea, Massachusetts (d. 1968)
Orville "Hoppy" Jones, American bass singer and cellist (The Ink Spots - "If I Didn't Care"), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1944)
Galo Plaza Lasso, Ecuadorian statesman (President of Ecuador 1948-52, head of OAS1968-75), born in New York City (d. 1987)
Ramon Tapales, Filipino composer and conductor, born in Baybay, Philippines (d. 1995)
Alec Wilder, American classical, jazz, and pop music composer (The Churkendoose), and musicologist (American Popular Song: The Great Innovators, 1900-1950), born in Rochester, New York (d. 1980)
Charles B. Timmer, Dutch translator and writer (Russia Black on White), born in Amsterdam (d. 1991)
